required.title: [Reference / Look Together] WALS Online (World Atlas of Language Structures)

suggested.title: [Listen] Short recordings or videos of the typologically diverse languages discussed

competence_markers.description: The student can classify a language by word order type from a brief structural description and predict associated features
The student can place a language on the morphological spectrum (analytic to polysynthetic) and explain what this means for learning strategy
The student can explain how case systems work and why they allow flexible word order
The student can compare tense-aspect-mood systems across at least three languages, identifying distinctions each language makes that others do not
The student can identify cognate families across related languages and can distinguish cognates from false friends
The student can produce a complete structural profile of a target language using the eight-point framework
The student can produce a formal structural comparison of English and a target language, identifying transfer points and interference risks
The student uses metalinguistic vocabulary (morpheme, syntax, inflection, case, aspect, typology) as working analytical tools, not memorized definitions

narration_prompts.detail: What are the major types? Which is most common? How does knowing a language's word order help you predict other features of its grammar?
What is the difference between an analytic language and an agglutinative one? Give examples. If you were learning each type, what would your strategy need to be?
Why do some languages need them? What does English use instead? What advantage does a case system give a language?

narration_prompts.prompt: Compare how two languages handle time in their verb systems.
narration_prompts.detail: What distinctions does one language make that the other does not? What does that tell you about what each language considers important?

narration_prompts.prompt: What are cognates and why are they useful?
narration_prompts.detail: What are false friends and why are they dangerous? Give examples of each.

narration_prompts.prompt: Present the structural profile of a language you have analyzed.
narration_prompts.detail: Walk me through each feature. How would this profile guide you if you were starting to learn this language?

cross_connections.description: Every structural feature analyzed in B2 is compared against the English baseline built in B1. The two packets are two halves of a single analytical project: B1 asks "how does English work?" and B2 asks "how do other languages work differently, and what does that reveal?"

Typological classification (B2) and genealogical classification (3.1.4) are complementary systems. Two languages can be typologically similar but genealogically unrelated (Turkish and Japanese are both SOV and agglutinative but are not related). Two languages can be genealogically related but typologically different (English and Hindi are both Indo-European but structurally very different). Understanding both classification systems gives a complete picture of linguistic diversity.

The spread of languages tracks the spread of civilizations. The student who understands why Romance languages exist (the Roman Empire spread Latin) and why Arabic is spoken from Morocco to Iraq (the Islamic conquests) integrates linguistic typology with civilizational history. Language and history illuminate each other.

The structural profile built in B2 directly accelerates the vocabulary acquisition in 3.1.3. The student who knows their target language is agglutinative with postpositions approaches vocabulary learning differently than if it were analytic with prepositions. Structural knowledge makes every word learned more efficient.
Pronoun systems that encode social hierarchy (B2, Part 5) connect directly to 3.1.5's study of power distance and formality in communication. The grammar is the cultural norm made structural.

The typological analysis in B2 is a classification system — organizing diverse phenomena into categories based on shared properties. This is the same cognitive operation that mathematical taxonomy performs. The student doing morphological classification is exercising the same reasoning as the student classifying geometric figures or number types.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you discovered that languages differ — different word orders, different question strategies, different time systems. In B1, you learned how English works at a structural level. Now you are going to do something much more powerful: you are going to build a systematic framework for analyzing any language on Earth.

This is not about learning to speak another language (that comes through 3.1.3's functional vocabulary and immersive practice). This is about understanding the architectural blueprints of language itself. Just as an architect who understands structural engineering can look at any building and understand how it stands up, you will be able to look at any language and understand how it organizes meaning.

The world's approximately 7,000 languages are not random. They cluster into types. Languages that put verbs at the end (like Japanese, Korean, Turkish, Hindi, and Latin) share other structural features — they tend to use postpositions instead of prepositions, they tend to put adjectives before nouns, and they tend to put dependent clauses before main clauses. Languages that put verbs in the middle (like English, French, Russian, and Swahili) have their own pattern of associated features. These correlations are not coincidence — they reflect deep organizing principles of human language.

Understanding these typological patterns gives you a structural map of linguistic diversity. When you encounter a new language, you will not start from zero. You will identify its type, predict its features, and build a structural profile before you learn a single word of vocabulary. This is the difference between a person who approaches every new language as a mystery and a person who approaches every new language with a map.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Word order typology** — the student can classify any language by its basic word order and predict associated structural features
2. **Morphological typology** — the student understands the spectrum from analytic to polysynthetic languages and can classify languages along it
3. **Case system comprehension** — the student understands how case systems work and why languages that have them can allow flexible word order
4. **Tense-aspect-mood analysis across languages** — the student can compare how different languages encode verbal meaning
5. **Pronoun systems and social encoding** — the student understands that pronouns carry social information that varies dramatically across languages
6. **Cognate identification** — the student can identify cognates across related languages and distinguish them from false friends
7. **Structural profiling** — the student can produce a structural profile of a target language, identifying its type and predicting its features
8. **The metalinguistic vocabulary** — the student acquires the technical terms (morpheme, phoneme, syntax, inflection, derivation, agreement, subordination) as working analytical tools
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Word Order Typology — The First Structural Question

When a linguist encounters a new language, the first question they ask is: what is the basic word order? Specifically, in a simple sentence with a subject, a verb, and an object (like "the dog bit the man"), which element comes first, second, and third?

There are six logically possible orders. All six exist in the world's languages, but they are not equally common:

**SOV (Subject-Object-Verb):** "The dog the man bit." The most common type globally — about 45% of languages. Japanese, Korean, Turkish, Hindi, Bengali, Urdu, Latin, Quechua, most languages of South America, and many others. The verb comes last. If a language is SOV, you always know the action word is at the end.

**SVO (Subject-Verb-Object):** "The dog bit the man." About 42% of languages. English, Mandarin Chinese, Russian, French, Spanish, Italian, Swahili, Malay, and many others. The verb sits between the doer and the receiver.

**VSO (Verb-Subject-Object):** "Bit the dog the man." About 9% of languages. Classical Arabic, Irish, Welsh, Tagalog (Filipino), Hawaiian. The verb comes first — the action is the first thing you hear.

**VOS (Verb-Object-Subject):** "Bit the man the dog." Rare — about 3%. Malagasy (Madagascar), some Austronesian and Mayan languages.

**OVS (Object-Verb-Subject):** "The man bit the dog." Very rare — fewer than 1%. Hixkaryana (Brazil) and a few other Amazonian languages.

**OSV (Object-Subject-Verb):** "The man the dog bit." Extremely rare. Warao (Venezuela) and very few others.

Here is where it gets powerful: **word order type predicts other structural features.** This is one of the most important discoveries in linguistics. SOV languages tend to:
- Use postpositions ("the house in") instead of prepositions ("in the house")
- Put adjectives before nouns
- Put possessors before the thing possessed ("dog's house" not "house of the dog")
- Put relative clauses before the noun they modify
- Put dependent clauses before main clauses

SVO languages tend to have the opposite pattern — prepositions, adjectives that can follow nouns, possessors after the possessed, relative clauses after the noun. These correlations are called **Greenberg's universals** (after the linguist Joseph Greenberg who first systematically documented them), and you will study them in depth at Standing Ground.

The practical implication is extraordinary: if you learn that a language is SOV, you can immediately predict a dozen other features of its grammar before you learn a single word. You have a structural map before you begin. This is what structural knowledge buys you.

### Part 2: Morphological Typology — How Languages Build Words

Languages differ not just in how they arrange words but in how much work individual words do. This is the morphological spectrum, and understanding where a language falls on it tells you what kind of learning it will require.

**Analytic (isolating) languages:** Words do not change form. Meaning is carried by word order and by separate, independent words. Mandarin Chinese is the clearest example. In Mandarin, the verb never changes — no conjugation, no tense endings, no agreement with the subject. "I go," "he go," "they go yesterday," "she go tomorrow" — the verb stays the same. Time, number, and relationships are expressed by separate words and by word order. Vietnamese and classical Chinese are also strongly analytic.

What this means for learning: analytic languages demand mastery of word order and particles. Grammar is about arrangement, not about memorizing word forms.

**Synthetic (inflectional) languages:** Meaning is carried partly by changes within words — endings, prefixes, or internal changes. Russian has six grammatical cases — each noun changes its ending depending on its role in the sentence (subject, object, indirect object, possessor, location, or instrument). Latin had the same system. German has four cases. Greek had five. In these languages, the word itself tells you its grammatical role, which means word order can be much more flexible — because the information that English carries through arrangement, these languages carry through word form.

What this means for learning: synthetic languages demand mastery of inflectional patterns. You need to know the case endings, the verb conjugations, the agreement rules. The upside is that once you know them, word order becomes a tool for emphasis rather than a carrier of basic meaning.

**Agglutinative languages:** Meaning is built by stacking pieces (morphemes) onto a root word in a regular, predictable way. Turkish is the textbook case. In Turkish, you can express "I will not be able to come" as a single word — each piece of meaning is a separate suffix added to the verb root in a fixed order. Finnish, Hungarian, Swahili, Japanese (partly), and Quechua are also agglutinative.

What this means for learning: agglutinative languages have large numbers of affixes, but each affix does one clear job. Once you know the pieces and the order, you can assemble and decode words systematically. The regularity is actually an advantage — there are fewer exceptions than in inflectional languages.

**Polysynthetic languages:** An entire sentence can be expressed as a single, enormously complex word. Inuktitut (Inuit), Mohawk, Navajo, and many other Indigenous American languages are polysynthetic. A single word in Inuktitut can mean what English would express as "I do not yet know how to build large houses" — all packed into one word through a chain of prefixes and suffixes.

What this means for learning: polysynthetic languages are the most structurally different from English. They require a completely different approach to sentence-level meaning — the unit of meaning is the word, and words are built through complex morphological processes that have no parallel in English.

### Part 3: Case Systems — When Words Announce Their Own Jobs

In English, you know who did what to whom by word order. "The dog bit the man" — the dog is the biter because it comes first. But many languages use a different system: they change the form of the noun itself to show its role. This is called **grammatical case**.

Consider Latin. "The dog bit the man" can be said in multiple word orders — the meaning does not change because the case endings on the nouns tell you their roles:

- *Canis* (nominative — subject) *virum* (accusative — object) *momordit* (verb) = the dog bit the man
- *Virum canis momordit* = the dog bit the man (same meaning, different word order)
- *Momordit canis virum* = the dog bit the man (same meaning, different word order)

The endings -is and -um tell you who is the biter and who is the bitten, regardless of where they appear in the sentence. Word order becomes a tool for emphasis, not a carrier of basic meaning.

English has vestigial case — you can see it in pronouns: "he" (subject/nominative), "him" (object/accusative), "his" (possessive/genitive). But English nouns do not change form by case. German has four cases. Russian has six. Finnish has fifteen. Hungarian has eighteen.

Understanding case systems is essential for two reasons. First, if you ever learn a case-rich language, you need to understand the concept before you encounter the forms. Second, understanding case helps you understand why English relies so heavily on word order — English lost most of its case endings during the Middle English period, and word order had to take over the work that case endings used to do.

### Part 4: Tense, Aspect, and Mood Across Languages

In B1, you learned that English verbs encode tense (when), aspect (how the action unfolds), mood (the speaker's attitude), and voice (active or passive). Now consider how wildly other languages handle these same dimensions.

**Mandarin Chinese** has no tense morphology at all. The verb never changes to show time. Instead, Mandarin uses time words ("yesterday," "tomorrow"), aspect particles (le for completed action, zhe for ongoing action, guo for experiential), and context. This is not a deficit — Mandarin can express every temporal nuance that English can. It just uses different tools.

**Russian** has only three tenses (past, present, future) but has a mandatory two-way aspect distinction that English does not have. Every Russian verb comes in two forms: perfective (the action is completed, bounded, viewed as a whole) and imperfective (the action is ongoing, repeated, or unbounded). "I read the book" (perfective — I finished it) versus "I was reading the book" (imperfective — I was in the middle of it) is a distinction that Russian grammar forces you to make every single time you use a verb. In English, the distinction exists but is often optional or ambiguous.

**Turkish** encodes evidentiality in the verb — whether the speaker witnessed the event personally or learned about it from someone else. "He came" in Turkish has two different forms: one meaning "he came (and I saw it)" and another meaning "he came (or so I heard)." English has no grammatical way to make this distinction — you have to add an entire clause: "I heard that he came." Turkish does it with a single suffix.

**Navajo** has an extraordinarily complex verb system that encodes information about the shape and nature of the object being handled. Different verb stems are used depending on whether the object is round, flat, long and rigid, long and flexible, or mushy. "Hand me the book" and "hand me the rope" use different verbs in Navajo — not because the action is different, but because the object has a different physical character.

These comparisons are not curiosities. They reveal that the categories English treats as fundamental (tense, for instance) are not universal, while categories English ignores (evidentiality, object shape) are grammatically mandatory in other languages. This is the structural insight that comparative grammar builds: **your language's categories are not the only possible categories, and other languages make distinctions that yours does not.**

### Part 5: Pronoun Systems and Social Architecture

Pronouns seem simple — he, she, it, they. But pronoun systems across languages reveal how societies encode social relationships directly into grammar.

**French** distinguishes "tu" (familiar, singular, intimate or casual) from "vous" (formal, plural, or respectful singular). The choice between them is not optional — it carries social meaning. Using "tu" with someone who expects "vous" is a social violation. Using "vous" with a close friend is cold and distancing.

**Japanese** has multiple first-person pronouns: "watashi" (neutral/formal), "boku" (casual, typically male), "ore" (very casual, masculine, potentially rough), "atashi" (feminine, casual), and others. Your choice of "I" announces your gender, your social register, and your relationship to the listener. English has one "I."

**Many Australian and Pacific languages** distinguish inclusive "we" (we including you) from exclusive "we" (we but not you). English "we" is ambiguous — "we should go" could mean "you and I should go" or "my group should go, and you're not part of it." Languages with this distinction cannot be ambiguous about it.

**Some languages** have no third-person pronouns at all — they use kinship terms, names, or titles instead. In these languages, referring to someone always involves specifying your relationship to them.

The lesson: pronoun systems are not just grammar — they are social architecture encoded in language. The structure of the language shapes what social information must be expressed and what can be left vague.

### Part 6: Cognates and False Friends

Languages that are related — that descended from the same ancestor language — share vocabulary. Words that share a common ancestor across languages are called **cognates**. Recognizing cognates is one of the most powerful accelerants in vocabulary acquisition.

The English word "night" is cognate with German "Nacht," Dutch "nacht," Swedish "natt," French "nuit," Spanish "noche," Italian "notte," Russian "noch," Hindi "raat" (more distantly), and many others. They all descend from a Proto-Indo-European root that meant the same thing thousands of years ago. If you know "night" in English and you learn to recognize the sound correspondences (English "n-ight" maps to German "N-acht," French "n-uit"), you can often guess related words in languages you have never studied.

But cognates have a dangerous cousin: **false friends** — words that look or sound similar across languages but mean different things. English "gift" means a present; German "Gift" means poison. English "embarrassed" looks like Spanish "embarazada," which means pregnant. English "sensible" looks like French "sensible," which means sensitive. The student who assumes similar-looking words always share meaning will make errors ranging from awkward to catastrophic.

The skill is learning to use cognates as accelerants while treating apparent similarities with appropriate suspicion until confirmed.

### Part 7: Building a Structural Profile

The capstone skill of this packet is the ability to produce a **structural profile** of any language — a systematic description of how it organizes meaning. A complete structural profile includes:

1. **Word order type** (SOV, SVO, VSO, etc.) and predicted associated features
2. **Morphological type** (analytic, synthetic, agglutinative, polysynthetic)
3. **Case system** (how many cases, if any, and what roles they mark)
4. **Verb system** (how tense, aspect, mood, and voice are expressed)
5. **Pronoun system** (what social distinctions are encoded)
6. **Question formation strategy** (word order change, particle, intonation, etc.)
7. **Negation pattern** (where does "not" go, and how is it expressed?)
8. **Relative clause strategy** (how does the language connect a description to a noun?)

This profile is a roadmap for learning. Instead of opening a textbook to "Chapter 1: Greetings," you begin with: "This is an SOV agglutinative language with six cases, postpositions, and a verb-final structure. My learning priority is the case endings and the agglutinative verb system, because those are where the language's structural logic lives."
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Word Order Classification [Early in the Packet]
Using WALS Online and the examples in the core content, the student classifies at least ten languages by word order type. For each, they predict what the associated features should be (prepositions or postpositions, adjective position, etc.) and then verify their predictions using WALS. The student creates a chart mapping languages to their word order types and notes where predictions were confirmed and where they were surprised.

### Study 2: Morphological Spectrum Analysis [Early-to-Mid Packet]
The student places at least eight languages on the morphological spectrum from analytic to polysynthetic, with brief justifications. They compare a single sentence ("The teacher gave the students new books") in at least three typologically different languages (one analytic, one synthetic, one agglutinative), examining how each language distributes meaning between word order, word form, and separate particles.

### Study 3: Case System Comparison [Mid-Packet]
The student compares the case systems of at least three languages: English (vestigial), German (four cases), and Russian or Finnish (six or fifteen cases). For each, they explain how the language marks the subject, direct object, indirect object, and possessor. The student demonstrates with examples how case-rich languages allow flexible word order while case-poor languages rely on fixed order.

### Study 4: Verb System Cross-Comparison [Mid-Packet]
The student compares how four languages express the same event across different temporal dimensions. Taking the sentence "I was reading the book when she arrived," the student examines how English, Mandarin, Russian, and Turkish each encode the temporal relationship, the aspectual distinction (ongoing vs. completed), and any additional information (evidentiality in Turkish). The comparison is presented as a chart with written analysis.

### Study 5: Cognate Detective Work [Mid-to-Late Packet]
The student identifies at least five cognate families across Indo-European languages (e.g., the "night/Nacht/nuit/noche/noch" family). For each, they trace the sound correspondences and note the regular patterns (English "f" corresponds to Latin/French "p" — father/pater, foot/ped, fish/piscis). The student also identifies at least three false friend pairs and explains the danger each poses.

### Study 6: Structural Profile — Target Language [Late Packet]
The student selects a target language (this may be the language they are studying through 3.1.3, or one that interests them) and produces a complete structural profile using the eight-point framework from Part 7. The profile uses information from reference sources (WALS, grammar descriptions, linguistic databases) and is presented as a written document with examples for each structural feature.

### Study 7: First Structural Comparison [End of Packet]
The student produces a formal written comparison of English and their target language along every structural dimension covered in the packet. For each dimension, they identify: where the languages are similar (transfer will help), where they differ (transfer will interfere), and what the learning priorities should be based on the structural differences. This is the capstone product of the packet.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Word Order Typology
**Developing well:** The student can classify languages by word order from descriptions or examples. They can explain Greenberg's correlations — what features tend to co-occur with each word order type — and use them to make predictions. They demonstrate understanding by correctly predicting features of a language based on its word order type.

**Developing slowly:** The student can name the six word order types but cannot explain the associated feature predictions. They treat word order classification as a label rather than a predictive tool.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student done the prediction-verification exercise — predicting features from word order and then checking against WALS? The predictive power of word order typology is what makes it meaningful, not the classification itself.

### 2. Morphological Typology
**Developing well:** The student can place languages on the morphological spectrum with clear justification, using specific examples (a single sentence rendered in an analytic, synthetic, and agglutinative language). They understand the implications for learning strategy — that an analytic language requires different study methods than an agglutinative one.

**Developing slowly:** The student memorizes which languages belong to which type but cannot explain why. They cannot produce examples showing the morphological difference in action. They do not connect the typology to practical learning implications.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student worked with actual language examples (translated and glossed sentences showing how each type distributes meaning), or only with abstract descriptions? The three-language sentence comparison in Study 2 is essential for making the spectrum concrete.

### 3. Cross-Linguistic Verb Analysis
**Developing well:** The student can compare verb systems across languages along all dimensions (tense, aspect, mood, evidentiality) and identify distinctions one language makes that another does not. They can articulate what these differences reveal about what each language "cares about" grammatically.

**Developing slowly:** The student describes each language's verb system in isolation but cannot produce meaningful comparisons. They list features without analyzing differences.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student done the parallel-sentence comparison (Study 4), examining the same event expressed in multiple languages? Side-by-side comparison forces the analytical thinking that isolated description does not.

### 4. Structural Profiling
**Developing well:** The student can produce a complete eight-point structural profile of a target language, with examples for each feature. The profile is specific, accurate, and could serve as a practical guide for beginning to learn the language. The student can explain how each structural feature would affect their learning strategy.

**Developing slowly:** The profile is incomplete or superficial — naming types without providing examples or connecting to learning implications. The student treats it as a fill-in-the-blank exercise rather than an analytical tool.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student used the profile to actually guide some language learning — even a small amount? A profile that remains purely theoretical has not demonstrated its purpose. Even spending one session using the profile to guide an initial approach to a target language makes its value concrete.

### 5. Metalinguistic Vocabulary
**Developing well:** The student uses technical terms (morpheme, phoneme, inflection, derivation, case, aspect, typology, cognate) naturally and accurately in their analysis. They can define terms when asked, but more importantly, they use them as working tools — the vocabulary serves the analysis, not the other way around.

**Developing slowly:** The student can define terms but does not use them in their own analysis. They write descriptions in everyday language when technical vocabulary would be more precise. Alternatively, they use terms without understanding them — sprinkling technical language for effect rather than accuracy.

**Diagnostic questions:** When the student uses a technical term, ask them to explain it. If they can, the vocabulary is real. If they falter, they are performing rather than understanding. Also check: does the student reach for technical vocabulary spontaneously when it would clarify their analysis, or do they only use it when prompted?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Typology as Stamp Collecting
If the student classifies languages into types but never uses the classifications to predict features, explain differences, or guide learning, the typology is inert knowledge. The point is not to label — it is to understand and predict. Every classification should lead to a "so what?" — what does this type tell us about how the language works and how to approach learning it?

### Exoticizing Non-English Languages
The temptation is to treat English as normal and everything else as exotic. "Turkish can express a whole sentence in one word — how weird!" This framing is both scientifically wrong (polysynthesis is well-represented globally and is no more "weird" than analytic structure) and educationally counterproductive (it reinforces the linguistic provincialism that 3.1.1 exists to dismantle). Frame diversity as variety, not as deviation.

### Oversimplifying the Morphological Spectrum
Real languages do not fit neatly into boxes. English is mostly analytic but has some synthetic features (verb conjugation, irregular plurals). Japanese is partly agglutinative and partly analytic. Mandarin is strongly analytic in morphology but has complex tonal and particle systems. Emphasize the spectrum nature of morphological typology — languages fall along a continuum, and most languages are mixed.

### Neglecting the Structural Comparison (Study 7)
The final structural comparison is the capstone of the packet, and it is the most demanding exercise. Do not let it be rushed or shallow. It should be a substantial written document — several pages — that demonstrates command of every analytical tool the packet has introduced. Allow sufficient time and require revisions.

### Disconnecting B2 from B1
B2 works only if the student has a solid English baseline from B1. Every cross-linguistic comparison implicitly involves English. If the student's English grammatical knowledge is shaky, the comparisons will be superficial. If you notice the student struggling with B2 comparisons, check whether the issue is actually an incomplete understanding of English structure — and address it through B1 reinforcement rather than pushing forward.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Word Order Typology
**Strong:** Explains all three major types (SOV, SVO, VSO) with examples and statistical distribution. Describes Greenberg's correlations — how word order predicts other features. Can apply the framework to a specific language.
**Weak:** Lists the types without examples or without explaining why the typology is useful. Cannot describe the correlated features.

### Prompt 2: Morphological Spectrum
**Strong:** Describes the full spectrum with clear examples. Explains the practical learning implications of each type. Uses a specific sentence comparison to illustrate the differences. Acknowledges that real languages are mixed.
**Weak:** Lists the four types with memorized definitions. Cannot explain what the typology means for learning. No concrete language examples.

### Prompt 3: Case Systems
**Strong:** Explains what grammatical case does (marks the noun's role), why some languages need it (to free up word order), and what English uses instead (fixed word order). Gives specific examples from at least two case-rich languages. Connects to the historical fact that English lost its cases.
**Weak:** Defines case abstractly without demonstrating understanding. Cannot explain the relationship between case and word order. No concrete examples.

### Prompt 4: Verb System Comparison
**Strong:** Compares specific dimensions (tense, aspect, evidentiality, etc.) across at least two languages with concrete examples. Identifies distinctions one language makes that the other does not. Reflects on what these differences reveal about how each language encodes reality.
**Weak:** Describes one language's verb system without genuine comparison. States that languages "do it differently" without specifying how.

### Prompt 5: Cognates and False Friends
**Strong:** Explains the concept clearly with multiple examples of cognate families and at least two false friend pairs. Describes the practical strategy — use cognates as accelerants but verify before assuming shared meaning.
**Weak:** Gives one example of each without developing the concept. Does not discuss strategy or practical implications.

### Prompt 6: Structural Profile Presentation
**Strong:** Walks through all eight profile dimensions with specific examples for each. Connects the profile to learning strategy — explains what they would prioritize in learning this language and why. Demonstrates genuine structural understanding, not just retrieved facts.
**Weak:** Incomplete profile. Reads like a list of facts rather than an integrated analysis. No connection to learning implications.
